Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal clause with metaphorical noun phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone pretending innocence after causing trouble
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from communal hunting culture where hunters concealed responsibility for failed expeditions
First Example Sentence: Ena kpọ r’efia vwo ọwọ gba, ama ọ r’eyẹ ruẹ kpẹ.
Second Example Sentence: Ovwe mẹ rẹ, ‘ọkpọ r’efia vwo ọwọ’ kẹ oghene rhiẹ.
Third Example Sentence: Oni ọmọ na kpọ r’efia vwo ọwọ nẹ oghọ ghọ.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To escalate conflict through reckless speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in Isoko oral dispute traditions
First Example Sentence: Ovwe emu kpọ ọkpọ kpọ ka ọrọ ghọ kpọsẹ.
Second Example Sentence: Mi rọ emu kpọ ọkpọ kpọ vẹ emọte.
Third Example Sentence: Ọ kpọ emu kpọ ọkpọ kpọ ne ogwa.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A deeply stubborn or shameless person
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to goat symbolism in Isoko agrarian life
First Example Sentence: Omote na ọkpọ kpẹ evu; ọ da rhe ọrọ.
Second Example Sentence: Ete rẹ kpọ ọkpọ kpẹ evu ne ugbo.
Third Example Sentence: Mi kie ọkpọ kpẹ evu nẹ ode.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone behaving beyond their social limits
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References traditional respect systems among Isoko elders
First Example Sentence: Ọrẹ avwọ kpọ sẹ udi ne ẹka oghọ.
Second Example Sentence: Elders kpọ avwọ kpọ sẹ udi ka omote ghọ.
Third Example Sentence: Mi da avwọ kpọ sẹ udi vẹ oghọ.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone trapped by their own lies
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used in moonlight folktales warning against dishonesty
First Example Sentence: Ọ kpọ kpọ n’akpo mgbe ọrọ fo.
Second Example Sentence: Mi rie ẹhẹ na ọkpọ kpọ n’akpo.
Third Example Sentence: Ovwe kpọ ọkpọ kpọ n’akpo nẹ market.

First Example Sentence: Ẹkpọ vwo ọkpọ ne oghene politics.
Second Example Sentence: Ọrọ ghọ jẹ ẹkpọ vwo ọkpọ kpẹ.
Third Example Sentence: Village ghọ rhiẹ ẹkpọ vwo ọkpọ.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Inspired by ritual purity beliefs in traditional Isoko spirituality
First Example Sentence: Obi rie evu kpọsẹ ne ọkpọ ghọ.
Second Example Sentence: Mi kie obi rie evu kpọsẹ nẹ rẹ.
Third Example Sentence: Oghene ma obi rie evu kpọsẹ.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Comes from communal labor expectations in farming settlements
First Example Sentence: Omote kpọ gbe ode ne ugbo.
Second Example Sentence: Mi da ọkpọ gbe ode vẹ project.
Third Example Sentence: Ena rẹ kpọ gbe ode mgbe nsogbu bia.

First Example Sentence: Ọrẹ avwe kpọ nẹ ashẹ ama ọ da ike.
Second Example Sentence: Mi kie avwe kpọ nẹ ashẹ ne football.
Third Example Sentence: Village ghọ ma avwe kpọ nẹ ashẹ.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional expression from village assemblies before judgments
First Example Sentence: Ẹkpọ da r’ukpe ne meeting ghọ.
Second Example Sentence: Mi rhiẹ ẹkpọ da r’ukpe mgbe elders bia.
Third Example Sentence: Omote ghọ ma ẹkpọ da r’ukpe kpẹ.
